exemplar.title: Exploring the Fourier series in Approximating Different Waveforms and Modelling Musical Chords

detailedExemplarData.title: Exploring the Fourier series in Approximating Different Waveforms and Modelling Musical Chords

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear sectioning into Aim, Background, Method, Derivations and Evaluation
Logical progression from data collection through Fourier derivation
Use of headings, tables and figure references to aid navigation
weaknesses: Frequent digressions into basic definitions and software choice reduce conciseness
Method description is truncated mid-sentence, obscuring procedure
Inconsistent in-text referencing and misplaced bibliography interrupt flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the submission is organized into recognizable sections and maintains logical development, unnecessary repetition, truncated descriptions and formatting hiccups limit coherence and conciseness. These issues place the work solidly in band 2 rather than band 3.

strengths: Effective use of equations, Desmos graphs and FFT screenshots to support explanations
Mostly consistent use of mathematical notation (Î£, Ï, aâ, bâ)
Step-by-step derivations with clear rationale for odd harmonic selection
weaknesses: Missing differentials (dx, du) in several integrals
Occasional small or unlabeled axes in figures hinder interpretation
Abrupt variable changes and under-justified substitutions create minor logical gaps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student employs multiple representations effectively and uses mathematical language correctly in most places. Minor notation inconsistencies and clarity issues prevent full accuracy, placing communication at band 3.

strengths: Personal connection to music demonstrates genuine interest and initiative
Creative modelling of a three-tone chord beyond standard single-wave examples
weaknesses: Mathematical pathway largely follows a textbook Fourier derivation
Limited novel insight beyond adapting known series
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is clear evidence of independent thinking and a personal approach in choosing instruments and constructing z(x), but the exploration remains fairly conventional. This aligns with band 2.

strengths: Meaningful discussion of how environmental noise and instrument properties affect results
Thoughtful suggestions for further study and recognition of calculation errors
weaknesses: Reflection lacks quantitative error analysis or numerical evaluation of discrepancies
Evaluation of outcomes remains largely qualitative without comprehensive future implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides thoughtful qualitative reflection and identifies methodological limitations, but the analysis is not deeply critical or quantitatively rigorous, fitting band 2.

strengths: Fourier series and harmonic analysis are highly relevant and linked to the research aim
Mathematics is commensurate with SL level and applied consistently
Derivations demonstrate good conceptual grasp of series formulation
weaknesses: Fundamental period miscalculated (440âHz instead of 1/440âs), leading to incorrect coefficient values
Inconsistent bounds labeling and omission of units (e.g., volts)
Key orthogonality arguments and substitution limits are under-explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Relevant and appropriately leveled mathematics underpins the exploration, but significant errors in period calculation and limits, along with missing justifications, limit the mark to band 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Arguments are usually presented step-by-step with brief justification (e.g. why odd harmonics only for square wave).  Some logical gaps exist (incorrect period calculation, abrupt variable changes), but overall clarity and structure are good.

Derivations of coefficients are mostly correct, yet include mis-handled limits, period confusion (b<1 error) and unverified series convergence.  Understanding is good with several small-to-moderate errors, aligning with band 3.

The exploration includes equations, algebraic derivations, Desmos graphs and screenshots of FFT output, demonstrating effective use of multiple representations to convey ideas.  They are generally well linked to the discussion, though axis labels are occasionally small.

The student organises the narrative into clear sections (Aim, Background, Method, Derivations, Evaluation), and the development from data collection to Fourier derivation follows a logical path.  Occasional digressions (e.g. extended discussion of instrument choice or basic definitions) interrupt flow, but the mathematical argument can still be followed without major difficulty.  Overall coherence is good rather than exemplary.

Adapting square and sawtooth series, experimenting with negative indices and altered parameters demonstrates creativity and initiative beyond a rote treatment, but results remain exploratory rather than exceptional.

Some analysis of discrepancies between model and data is provided, referencing amplitude, instrument friction, etc.  The discussion is thoughtful but lacks rigorous quantitative error analysis, so it fits the âthoughtfulâ band.

Outcomes are evaluated qualitatively; the student recognises limitations and proposes future investigations.  Evaluation is clear but not comprehensive with numerical validation, so band 2 is appropriate.

The student reflects on why approximations only match peaks, discusses environmental noise, and suggests further studies, showing meaningful reflection throughout.  The depth is moderate, not fully critical.

Mathematical symbols (Î£, Ï, a_n, b_n), integral notation and trigonometric identities are used appropriately in most derivations.  Minor inconsistencies (switching between x and t, missing differential in a few integrals) prevent full accuracy, hence âmostly consistentâ.

The studentâs choice of instruments, use of Friture, and self-directed comparison of approximations reveal a clear personal approach.  However, execution remains fairly conventional and not highly innovative, matching band 2.

While most content is related to modelling musical waveforms, many passages repeat earlier explanations of sine properties or describe software choice in detail, making the exploration longer than necessary.  Relevance is generally maintained but conciseness is only moderate.

Fourier series, harmonic analysis and trigonometric modelling are highly relevant to the aim and are used in multiple sections.  Despite some detours, mathematics is consistently linked to the problem, warranting effective use (band 4).

Collecting personal sound data, choosing own chord, and attempting bespoke approximations show significant independent thinking, yet the mathematical pathway largely follows standard textbook Fourier derivations without novel insight, placing the work in the âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ category.

A recognizable introduction-body-conclusion structure is present and figures are referenced in sequence.  Headings and tables aid navigation.  Some sections (e.g. Appendix A bibliography placed mid-text) reduce polish, so organisation is âwell organisedâ but not âexceptionallyâ so.

Fourier series sits comfortably within HL syllabus; the student extends to piece-wise derivations and summations, matching course level completely.  Minor conceptual slips prevent a higher âcomprehensiveâ score.

annotations.comment: The Desmos-generated plots for square and sawtooth waves effectively illustrate hypothetical waveforms and support the theoretical discussion.
Figures 4 and 5 lack axis labels and units. Adding clear labels will improve interpretation of the Desmos plots.
Integrals in the derivation are presented without differentials (e.g., missing dx). Include dx consistently to meet mathematical communication standards.
The studentâs personal connection to music in the Background effectively demonstrates genuine interest and independent thinking in choosing this topic.

Background paragraphs include general real-world applications but lack a clear link to the stated aim. Consider streamlining this section to focus only on material directly supporting the research question.
The Method description is truncated mid-sentence, making the intended procedure unclear. Ensure complete sentences and fully describe each step to improve coherence.
The explanation of why the viola cannot play three notes simultaneously is somewhat confusing. Clarify this limitation and link it directly to how it affects data collection.
Figure 2âs axis labels are very small in the FFT screenshot, which may hinder interpretation. Consider enlarging or annotating axes to improve clarity.
Including the screenshot of Friture output effectively shows multiple representations of the sound wave and supports the methodological description.
The text states Fâ350 Hz for the F major note, yet the standard pitch F4 is 349.23 Hz. Clarify your measured value versus the theoretical frequency and rounding.
The description that peaks and troughs represent voltage maxima/minima lacks consistent units. Explicitly state measurement units (e.g., V) each time you refer to amplitude.
The transition from recording results to introducing the summation in Section 5 could be smoother. Use a brief signpost sentence to guide the reader to the derivation.
The citation âScienceDirect, 2021â interrupts the narrative flow. Adopt a consistent in-text referencing style and include full references in the bibliography.
The explanation uses limits Â±Ïn for u but originally bounds were Â±L. This inconsistency can confuse the reader; align bounds clearly.
In the expansion of the sine-product integral, include the differential du in the U-substitution step to maintain rigorous notation.
The derivation of aâ is presented clearly and follows logical steps, providing a solid foundation for subsequent coefficient calculations.
The switch from general L to setting L=Ï for the period requires clearer justification; explicitly connect half-period reasoning to L=Ï.
The step eliminating the cosine integral when mâ n is not explained. A brief note on orthogonality of cosine terms would strengthen the argument.
The U-substitution derivation switches between x and u without explicitly stating the relationship of the limits. Clearly define new limits after substitution.
While you note that the integral of sin(u) vanishes over symmetric bounds, a brief explanation of the area-cancellation principle would improve clarity.
Numbering displayed equations (e.g., (1), (2)) would improve navigation and cross-referencing throughout the derivation.
The summary table of aâ, aâ and bâ formulae is concise and provides an effective visual reference for the derived expressions.
The tableâs cell layout overlaps content and column headers; improve formatting so that each formula aligns with its heading clearly.
In Table 1âs caption, explicitly define L as half the period to avoid reader confusion over its meaning.
The period is miscalculated as 440 rather than 1/440 s. This fundamental error requires correction and explanation of the reciprocal relationship.
The computed value of bâ0.0143 uses an incorrect period value. Recalculate using period=1/440 to obtain b=2ÏÃ440.
When assuming a cosine graph (no phase shift), explicitly discuss the horizontal translation and phase parameter to justify this choice.
The plotted result a(x) appearing as a near-horizontal line signals a conceptual error. Reflect quantitatively on how parameter misinterpretation led to this outcome.
The student recognizes the calculation error and reevaluates the period, showing critical reflection on their methodology.
Combining three cosine functions to model the F major chord demonstrates clear initiative in constructing z(x) and goes beyond rote computation.
Assuming the same amplitude n for each cosine term in z(x) lacks justification. Explain how you chose equal amplitudes across different frequencies.
